content.text: Handoffs in the OpenAI Agents SDK

is the mechanism by which one agent transfers control — and the conversation history — to another. The originating agent stays in scope; the SDK routes the next model call to the target agent and continues the agentic loop from there. The final result always surfaces through the same
the caller received, so handoffs are transparent to the application layer.
: a lightweight triage agent decides which expert to call, and each expert focuses solely on its domain without knowing about the others.

attrs.alt: OpenAI Agents SDK handoff mechanics diagram

Replaces the active agent with the target agent and continues the agentic loop.
field identifies the agent that produced the final response.
The SDK does not start a new run for each handoff. It re-uses the same loop, replacing the active agent. This means a single call to
can transparently chain through N handoffs.
The most common pattern: pass a list of target agents directly to

from agents import Agent, Runner

billing_agent = Agent(
    name="BillingSupport",
    instructions="Handle billing questions, refunds, and invoice queries.",
)

tech_agent = Agent(
    name="TechSupport",
    instructions="Resolve technical issues and explain product features.",
)

triage_agent = Agent(
    name="TriageBot",
    instructions=(
        "Determine whether the user has a billing question or a technical question. "
        "Use transfer_to_BillingSupport for billing issues and "
        "transfer_to_TechSupport for technical issues."
    ),
    handoffs=[billing_agent, tech_agent],
)

result = Runner.run_sync(triage_agent, "I was charged twice for my subscription.")
print(result.last_agent.name)   # "BillingSupport"
print(result.final_output)
factory function when you need to customise how the handoff appears to the model or attach a callback.

from agents import Agent, handoff, RunContextWrapper

def alert_on_escalation(ctx: RunContextWrapper) -> None:
    # called just before the SDK switches to the escalation agent
    print(f"[ALERT] Escalating run {ctx.run_id} to human review")

escalation_agent = Agent(
    name="EscalationAgent",
    instructions="Apologise and tell the user a human will be in touch within 24 hours.",
)

support_agent = Agent(
    name="SupportBot",
    instructions="Help users. Escalate if you cannot resolve the issue.",
    handoffs=[
        handoff(
            escalation_agent,
            tool_name_override="escalate_to_human",
            tool_description_override=(
                "Transfer to a human agent when the issue cannot be resolved automatically."
            ),
            on_handoff=alert_on_escalation,
        ),
    ],
)
The target agent to hand off to
Custom function name exposed to the model (default: transfer_to_{agent.name})
Override the description the model uses to decide when to hand off
Callback fired with RunContextWrapper just before the switch
Transform the message list passed to the target agent
By default, the full conversation history is passed to the target agent. Use
to prune, summarise, or inject context before the handoff.

from agents import handoff
from agents.handoffs import HandoffInputData

def keep_last_three(data: HandoffInputData) -> HandoffInputData:
    """Pass only the last three user/assistant turns to the target agent."""
    recent = data.input_history[-6:]   # 3 turns × 2 messages (user + assistant)
    return HandoffInputData(
        input_history=recent,
        pre_handoff_items=data.pre_handoff_items,
        new_items=data.new_items,
    )

specialist = Agent(name="Specialist", instructions="Answer the user's question.")

triage = Agent(
    name="Triage",
    instructions="Route to Specialist for detailed questions.",
    handoffs=[handoff(specialist, input_filter=keep_last_three)],
)

from agents.extensions.handoff_filters import remove_all_tools

# Strip all tool-call and tool-result messages from history before handoff.
# Useful when the target agent should not see the triage agent's internal tool usage.
triage = Agent(
    name="Triage",
    instructions="Route to Specialist.",
    handoffs=[handoff(specialist, input_filter=remove_all_tools)],
)
When the model hands off, it can supply structured data via the handoff tool's input. Define an
to receive this data in the

from pydantic import BaseModel
from agents import Agent, handoff, RunContextWrapper

class EscalationData(BaseModel):
    reason: str
    urgency: str   # "low" | "medium" | "high"

def handle_escalation(ctx: RunContextWrapper, data: EscalationData) -> None:
    print(f"Escalation reason: {data.reason}, urgency: {data.urgency}")
    # route to PagerDuty, Slack, ticket system, etc.

escalation_agent = Agent(
    name="EscalationAgent",
    instructions="Acknowledge the escalation and confirm a human will follow up.",
)

support_agent = Agent(
    name="SupportBot",
    instructions=(
        "Help users. If the issue is unresolvable, call escalate_to_human with "
        "the reason and urgency level."
    ),
    handoffs=[
        handoff(
            escalation_agent,
            tool_name_override="escalate_to_human",
            input_type=EscalationData,
            on_handoff=handle_escalation,
        ),
    ],
)

Handoffs chain naturally. An agent that receives a handoff can itself hand off to a further specialist. The SDK continues the agentic loop until an agent produces a final response with no outstanding tool calls.

from agents import Agent, Runner

level3 = Agent(
    name="LegalReview",
    instructions="Provide a brief legal disclaimer and refer the user to a lawyer.",
)

level2 = Agent(
    name="PolicyExpert",
    instructions=(
        "Answer policy questions. If the question has legal implications, "
        "hand off to LegalReview."
    ),
    handoffs=[level3],
)

level1 = Agent(
    name="GeneralSupport",
    instructions="Answer general questions. Route policy questions to PolicyExpert.",
    handoffs=[level2],
)

result = Runner.run_sync(level1, "Can I legally resell your software?")
print(result.last_agent.name)    # "LegalReview" — two hops from level1
print(result.final_output)
Circular handoffs — where agent A can hand off to B, and B can hand off back to A — are technically possible but dangerous. They can consume the entire
budget without making progress. Design handoff graphs as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) where possible.
A single triage agent routes to N specialists. Specialists never hand off further — they handle or escalate to a human queue.

specialists = [billing_agent, tech_agent, account_agent]

hub = Agent(
    name="Triage",
    instructions=(
        "Classify the request as billing, technical, or account-related, "
        "then immediately hand off to the correct specialist. "
        "Do not attempt to answer the question yourself."
    ),
    handoffs=specialists,
)
Each agent tries to answer and falls back to the next if it cannot. The last agent in the chain always answers, even if imperfectly.

human_fallback = Agent(
    name="HumanFallback",
    instructions="Apologise and confirm a human agent will respond within 24 hours.",
)

specialist = Agent(
    name="Specialist",
    instructions="Answer technical questions. If uncertain, hand off to HumanFallback.",
    handoffs=[human_fallback],
)

first_responder = Agent(
    name="FirstResponder",
    instructions="Handle simple FAQs. For complex technical questions, hand off to Specialist.",
    handoffs=[specialist],
)
After a run, check which agent produced the final answer and trace the handoff path.
Listing the same agent twice in
No explicit routing instructions — the model needs to know which tool to call and when. Be specific in
Expecting handoffs to work without history — target agents receive the conversation history from the originating agent. If that history is large, use
to prune it and save tokens.
Circular graphs without turn limits — always set a conservative
callback can be async. This is useful when you need to perform I/O — logging to a database, publishing a metric, or calling an external service — before the target agent takes over.

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner, handoff, RunContextWrapper

async def log_handoff_async(ctx: RunContextWrapper) -> None:
    # simulate async I/O (e.g. write to a logging service)
    await asyncio.sleep(0)
    print(f"[LOG] Handoff fired; run_id={ctx.run_id}")

escalation = Agent(name="Escalation", instructions="Acknowledge and close.")

agent = Agent(
    name="Support",
    instructions="Help users. Escalate unresolvable issues.",
    handoffs=[handoff(escalation, on_handoff=log_handoff_async)],
)

async def main():
    result = await Runner.run(agent, "This is unresolvable.")
    print(result.final_output)

asyncio.run(main())
Because agents are immutable dataclasses, you can test handoff routing without calling the real LLM by replacing the model with a mock that deterministically emits the handoff tool call.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock
from agents import Agent, Runner, handoff

billing = Agent(name="Billing", instructions="Handle billing.")
tech = Agent(name="Tech", instructions="Handle tech.")

triage = Agent(
    name="Triage",
    instructions="Route to the right agent.",
    handoffs=[billing, tech],
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
)

def test_billing_route(monkeypatch):
    # Confirm that a billing question ends up at the billing agent.
    # In a real test suite you would mock the model layer; here we just
    # verify the structure is correct.
    names = {a.name for a in triage.handoffs}
    assert "Billing" in names
    assert "Tech" in names

def test_handoff_tool_names():
    from agents.handoffs import Handoff
    h = handoff(billing, tool_name_override="go_to_billing")
    assert h.tool_name == "go_to_billing"
Two approaches exist for multi-agent patterns:
(code-driven). Choosing the right one affects latency, cost, and debuggability.

# The model decides which agent to call.
# Good for open-ended routing where the correct path varies by user message.

triage = Agent(
    name="Triage",
    instructions="Route to the correct specialist.",
    handoffs=[billing, tech],
)
result = Runner.run_sync(triage, user_message)

# You decide which agent to call based on application logic.
# Good for deterministic workflows where the path is known in advance.

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner

classifier = Agent(name="Classifier", instructions="Reply with exactly one word: billing or tech.")

async def route(user_message: str) -> str:
    label_result = await Runner.run(classifier, user_message)
    label = label_result.final_output.strip().lower()

    if label == "billing":
        specialist = billing
    else:
        specialist = tech

    final = await Runner.run(specialist, user_message)
    return final.final_output

print(asyncio.run(route("I was charged twice.")))
Use handoffs when the routing logic is ambiguous or context-dependent. Use explicit chaining when the path is deterministic and you want full control over which agent is called and with what input.
One additional model call for triage
Two separate model calls; no triage overhead
Easier — routing is explicit in code
Open-ended customer support, unknown request types
callback on every handoff that records the source agent, target agent, and a timestamp. This creates an audit trail for support and debugging.
— each handoff passes the full conversation history by default. In long sessions, use
to cap history length and keep token costs predictable.
) that can handle anything. Without it, the triage agent may loop if no specialist matches.
may fire more than once if the SDK retries a turn. Make callbacks idempotent (e.g. use upsert instead of insert when writing to a database).
Handoffs let a triage agent delegate to specialists without the caller needing to know how many agents are involved
